elements.sent-2411-001: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong abilities in common-sense reasoning and interactive decision-making, but often struggle with complex, long-horizon planning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: Recent techniques have sought to structure LLM outputs using control flow and other code-adjacent techniques to improve planning performance.
elements.sent-2411-003: These techniques include using variables (to track important information) and functions (to divide complex tasks into smaller re-usable sub-tasks).
elements.sent-2411-004: However, purely code-based approaches can be error-prone and insufficient for handling ambiguous or unstructured data.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address these challenges, we propose \ours, an LLM planning approach that is fully code-expressive (it can utilize all the benefits of code) while also being dynamic (it can flexibly adapt from errors and use the LLM for fuzzy situations).
elements.sent-2411-006: In \ours, an LLM solves tasks by interacting with a Read-Eval-Print Loop (REPL), which iteratively executes and evaluates code, similar to language shells or interactive code notebooks, allowing the model to flexibly correct errors and handle tasks dynamically.
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate that \ours achieves strong results across various planning domains compared to previous methods.
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[trim={20 55 20 60}, width=\textwidth]{figures/overview_v2.pdf} \end{center} \caption{\ours is an approach for augmenting LLM-planning by using LLMs to interact with \oursrepls, which are an extension of REPLs (Read-Eval-Print-Loops, e.g. language shells, code notebooks). \fpart{A} Observations are printed out in the main \oursrepl and all child \oursrepls. \fpart{B} \fpart{C} The LLM, which outputs code in the \oursrepl line by line, interacts with the environment by calling the act function. Each \oursrepl (including child) can directly act on the environment. \fpart{D} The LLM can ``spawn'' child \oursrepls by calling undefined functions. These functions can be used to abstract various parts of the task (e.g.\ finding one of the necessary objects, interpreting if the current location is closed, etc.). \fpart{E} Arguments and return values are passed between parent/child \oursrepls using the get\_args and answer functions. \fpart{F} \oursrepls can be called multiple times, and continue output from the last answer statement. This allows an LLM to output consistent and correct outputs. \fpart{G} An LLM can combine these tools (child \oursrepls, code control flow) to express complex workflows that can solve tasks in a compact way. } \label{fig:overview} \vafterfig
elements.sent-2411-008: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong capabilities in planning and decision making tasks~\citep{wang2023survey, xi2023rise .}
elements.sent-2411-009: LLMs have strong domain-knowledge and common-sense priors~\citep{kojima2023largelanguagemodelszeroshot,brown2020languagemodelsfewshotlearners , which are required in many of these planning tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-010: For instance, answering ``What steps should I take to prepare dinner?'' requires knowledge about food preparation and the kitchen environment.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, LLMs are imperfect and can struggle to make accurate decisions for complex and long-horizon tasks --- often making ``hallucinations'' and/or incorrect short-term decisions~\citep{kambhampati2024llms, valmeekam2023planningabilitieslargelanguage, liu2023llmpempoweringlargelanguage .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Prior work has improved planning performance by guiding an LLM by enforcing levels of structure to the LLM's outputs and decisions.
elements.sent-2411-013: A prominent method of guiding LLMs is to explicitly prompt the LLM to ``think step by step''~\citep{yao2022react, kojima2022large , which causes the LLM to output statements to break down the given planning task into smaller tasks, or sub-tasks, and ``think'' through its decisions while planning.}
elements.sent-2411-014: Prior work has also guided LLM-planning by enforcing explicit data structures, such as dividing the task into lists of sub-tasks and trees of sub-tasks~\citep{yao2023tree, schroeder2024threadthinkingdeeperrecursive , then recursively planning on these sub-tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Prior work has also structured LLM decisions by using code, which we call here code-augmented LLM-planning.
elements.sent-2411-016: As recent LLMs have been trained on large code bases~\citep{chen2021evaluatinglargelanguagemodels,yang2024llmwizardcodewand , LLMs have the ability to utilize the control flow structures of code to plan accurately.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Some works prompt an LLM to write code or pseudocode solely to help the LLM self-structure its decisions, without explictly executing code~\citep{chae2024language .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Other works prompt an LLM to write code that is directly executed to solve the given task~\citep{gao2023palprogramaidedlanguagemodels, wang2023voyageropenendedembodiedagent .}
elements.sent-2411-019: However, using LLM-written code also introduces the inherent limitations of code to planning.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this work, we examine the following limitations: (1) ``Fuzzy'' sub-problems: many tasks require a planner to answer or solve some ``fuzzy'' task that is not easily solved using code.
elements.sent-2411-021: A planner may need to interpret unstructured observation data, or make decisions on possibly subjective choices (e.g.\ ``purchase the item that best matches the user's request'').
elements.sent-2411-022: This includes writing functions for sub-processes before writing code to solve the main tasks and writing case statements that must consider every possible output of a function or result of a sub-task.
elements.sent-2411-023: Generating accurate code in this manner requires precise and accurate forethought.
elements.sent-2411-024: In this work, we seek to solve these issues by creating a top-down framework for code-augmented LLM-planning that is both dynamic and code-expressive.
elements.sent-2411-025: In particular, we take inspiration from how real human developers write and prototype code, with REPLs (Read-Eval-Print-Loops), and other interactive computing paradigms~\citep{steele1996evolution .}
elements.sent-2411-026: A REPL, also known as a language shell, or an interactive code notebook (e.g. IPython, Jupyter), is a coding environment wherein a programmer inputs a single statement of code, the environment executes the code and displays the output to the programmer, and then the programmer repeats the process with a new statement of code.
elements.sent-2411-027: Using a REPL allows a programmer to write code step-by-step, see the results at each step, and correct their mistakes while interacting with the REPL.
elements.sent-2411-028: We introduce \ours: an approach for solving complex planning problems (shown in \Cref{fig:overview ) by using LLMs to interact with \oursrepls, where the LLM writes code line-by-line, writing and calling functions to interact with the planning environment.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Specifically, \oursrepls are an extension of REPLs --- in addition to REPL functionality, code in \oursrepls can recursively ``spawn'' child \oursrepls.
elements.sent-2411-030: This recursive spawning allows the planner to solve tasks top-down, and can be used to abstract various subtasks and ``fuzzy'' sub-problems.
elements.sent-2411-031: Our work makes the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-032: We show that \ours achieves strong performance in challenging text-based sequential decision making environments ALFWorld~\citep{shridhar20alfworld and WebShop~\citep{yao2022webshop .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: We also show \ours achieves strong performance on a novel real-world web navigation task that requires handling complex web observations.
elements.sent-2411-034: We test the robustness of \ours by conducting ablations --- testing the code correction/prediction ability by giving the LLM faulty demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-035: In a typical interactive planning task, a large language model agent (LLM-agent) is given a task description $t$ and must interact with an environment for a number of timesteps to accomplish the task $t$.
elements.sent-2411-036: At each timestep $i$, the agent is given a language observation $o_i$ of the current state of the environment and can predict a language action $a_i$ which can affect the environment (for the agent to accomplish $t$).
elements.sent-2411-037: To help the LLM-agent, it is given $k$-trajectories (or demonstrations) $\{\tau_1, \ldots, \tau_k\}$ of solving some prior tasks that are created by an expert.
elements.sent-2411-038: The LLM-agent must follow the demonstrations to solve the current task $t$.
elements.sent-2411-039: A simple way to apply the LLM is to simply model the next action using the language-modeling probabilities of the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-040: However, many real-world tasks are complex --- they may require solving multiple smaller sub-tasks, and long-horizon --- they require many action predictions to solve the task.
elements.sent-2411-041: Enforcing structure, such as writing code, to the LLM-agent's decisions, can be used to address these issues.
elements.sent-2411-042: In this work, we propose a novel method for using code to augment LLM-agents, which addresses the limitations of using code with planning, described in~\Cref{sec:intro : (1) ``Fuzzy'' sub-problems, (2) planning bottom-up, and finally (3) coding errors.}

elements.sent-2411-043: We base our approach on the concept of a REPL (Read-Eval-Print-Loop), also known as a language shell or code notebook, is a programming environment that tracks program state (global/local variables) at each timestep where (1) a user inputs a statement of code (variable assignment, loop, function definition), (2) this line of code is evaluated, to obtain the next program state, (3) the output of the previous line of code is shown to the user, and finally (4) repeat step (1).
elements.sent-2411-044: A REPL is useful for task solving in a dynamic manner --- results (and possible errors) are immediately reflected in the output of the program state.
elements.sent-2411-045: Then, a user (or LLM), can simply write another statement of code that can correct their previous mistake, without re-writing the entire program.
elements.sent-2411-046: We extend the idea of using a REPL recursively --- where any REPL can spawn another REPL in a top-down manner, in order to solve any (possibly fuzzy) sub-task --- a ``subtask'' REPL.
elements.sent-2411-048: In \Cref{sec:ablations , we show experimentally adding recursive spawning is crucial for solving tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-049: To create a \oursrepl, we simply add pre-defined functions, or primitives, to the program state that can recursively spawn child \oursrepls, and manage context-passing between parent-child \oursrepls.
elements.sent-2411-050: The LLM is then queried using the [subtask] REPL until context is passed back to this REPL or another child REPL is spawned.
elements.sent-2411-051: If the [subtask] \oursrepl has previously been spawned, then execution history will continue from previous.
elements.sent-2411-052: Otherwise, a task description for the \oursrepl will be queried, and a new \oursrepl will be created.
elements.sent-2411-053: Note the new child \oursrepl does not share any variable state with the parent, so the following primitives can be used to pass context.
elements.sent-2411-054: Execution is passed back to the parent.
elements.sent-2411-055: Pseudo-code for how a practical implementation of \oursrepls is given in \Cref{sec:impl-details .}
elements.sent-2411-056: As all the planning and actions can be done in a \oursrepl, \ours is code-expressive.
elements.sent-2411-057: To execute \ours, we can adapt an \oursrepl by adding more primitive functions for interacting with the environment.
elements.sent-2411-058: This may be used by an planner to execute some string processing code when an observation is too large (e.g.\ a whole web page).
elements.sent-2411-059: For each timestep $i$, we run the \oursrepl until the act(a) function is called, then pass in any observations as needed to the in-code-environment-outputs of the \oursrepls.
elements.sent-2411-060: We demonstrate how LLM calls, code, and actions from act are executed across different parent-child \oursrepls in \Cref{tab:cxt-pass .}
elements.sent-2411-061: To use \ours in multiple planning tasks, we keep a global REPL pool, a set of spawned REPLs from previous task executions and demonstrations.
elements.sent-2411-062: Note that an \oursrepl, when called or spawned, re-uses the code/output history.
elements.sent-2411-063: In this way, an LLM can perform in-context-learning from previous tasks and demonstrations to output correct and consistent code.
elements.sent-2411-064: We evaluated \ours against baselines on various language-based environments.
elements.sent-2411-065: Each of these environments is challenging --- to be successful, an agent must be able to understand and predict long-horizon complex action sequences, and have enough language-understanding to interpret the language observations and actions.
elements.sent-2411-066: We show that \ours is an effective balance in using code for planning --- using code to structure complex, repeating, and language-understanding sub-tasks.
elements.sent-2411-067: We tested on the following environments:.
elements.sent-2411-068: ALFWorld~\citep{shridhar20alfworld is a text-based simulated-household embodied agent environment, where an agent must accomplish 6 different types of tasks related to navigating and interacting with a household, e.g. searching the kitchen for an apple, heating it, then placing it on a table.}
elements.sent-2411-069: WebShop~\citep{yao2022webshop is an interactable e-commerce environment, where an agent is given a target product description---in which a human annotator described a category, key attributes, and price limit---and the agent must navigate the e-commerce site by searching for and buying a product that matches the target description.}
elements.sent-2411-070: An agent's success is measured based on how many of the purchased item's attributes match with the ground truth attributes.
elements.sent-2411-071: In WebShop, the search page is configured to return the top $k$ results from the search query.
elements.sent-2411-072: Prior approaches have set $k=3$, and tested strategies where the best item is selected only from the first page (Top-3).
elements.sent-2411-073: We additionally test a setting where $k=10$.
elements.sent-2411-074: In this setting, we tested the strongest baseline, THREAD~\citep{schroeder2024threadthinkingdeeperrecursive ---an LLM approach that uses a text-based divide-and-conquer strategy--- by (1) using the same Top-3 strategy, and (2) using a Top-20 strategy --- searching twice and selecting the best among 20.}
elements.sent-2411-076: To test the scalability of \ours, we further designed a set of real-world web navigation tasks on actual websites.
elements.sent-2411-077: These tasks are difficult as actual websites can be extremely large (4k-20k tokens, compared to $\leq 500$ tokens per web-page in WebShop).
elements.sent-2411-078: We tested the agents on distributions of tasks where the goal is to navigate an E-commerce website and add item(s) that match a given description to the cart.
elements.sent-2411-079: We divided the tasks into a ``simple'' category, where the agent only needs to add an item of any description (e.g.\ add any laptop to the cart from bestbuy.com) and a ``complex'' category, where the agent must add as many items as it can find that match a specific description (e.g.\ loop through each search result and add every printer that is capable of printing at least 12 pages per minute to the cart).
elements.sent-2411-080: The full task descriptions and examples of observations and actions can be found in \Cref{sec:rww-details .}
elements.sent-2411-081: We find that elements of top-down recursive, dynamic, and code-expressivity are important for approaches to score a high success rate on all environments (top-down recursive and dynamic for ALFWorld, dynamic and code-expressivity for WebShop and Real-World Web).
elements.sent-2411-082: For example, the baseline THREAD~\citep{schroeder2024threadthinkingdeeperrecursive has top-down recursive and dynamic elements, but not code-expressivity, has high success rate on ALFWorld and $k = 3$ WebShop, but fails to scale in $k = 10$ WebShop and Real-World Web.}

elements.sent-2411-083: We show the results of testing approaches on ALFWorld in \Cref{tab:alfworld .}
elements.sent-2411-084: Our approach, \ours, achieves the highest success rate (SR) of 97.0\% compared to the best baseline, THREAD~\citep{schroeder2024threadthinkingdeeperrecursive , with 95.5\% SR.}
elements.sent-2411-085: We find that top-down recursive and dynamic approaches are crucial to solving this task, which ours and THREAD both have.
elements.sent-2411-086: Both \ours and THREAD effectively divide-and-conquer various sub-tasks of ALFWorld (e.g.\ dividing a task into (1) finding an apple, (2) heating it, then (3) placing it on the table), and are able to effectively change plans if there are environment issues (e.g. object is misplaced or mis-referenced).
elements.sent-2411-087: We show the results of various approaches on different LLMs, settings, and strategies on WebShop in \Cref{tab:webshop .}
elements.sent-2411-088: For simple strategies such as Top-3, i.e. simply searching and then comparing the Top-3 items, many baselines score a high ($\geq 46$\%) success rate: RAP, AutoGuide, THREAD, and \ours, with THREAD reaching the the highest SR of 49\%.
elements.sent-2411-089: However, this strategy is simplistic and limited --- trajectories can be completed within 10 actions, and success primarily relies on the LLM's ability to understand web-page observations.
elements.sent-2411-090: By testing on the Page $k=10$ setting with GPT4o-mini on \ours and the strongest baseline THREAD, we find that a combination of top-down recursive and code-expressive approaches are valuable in attaining a high SR in this setting and being able to successfully execute a Top-20 strategy.
elements.sent-2411-091: We show the results of running ReACT, THREAD, and \ours in the Real-World Web Environment in \Cref{tab:rwweb .}
elements.sent-2411-092: We find that each agent is able to solve tasks from the Simple distribution (tasks outlined in ~\Cref{sec:rww-details ).}
elements.sent-2411-093: These tasks are short horizon and can be completed in 5 actions.
elements.sent-2411-094: However, they still require the agent to be able to manage and interpret the long web page observations, which can be 4k-20k tokens long.
elements.sent-2411-095: Despite multiple attempts to improve THREAD described in ~\Cref{sec:thread-mods , we found THREAD could not effectively plan when there are long observation contexts.}
elements.sent-2411-096: We also give a qualitative analysis of this result in \Cref{fig:rww-qualitative .}
elements.sent-2411-097: In this analysis, we show why \ours is able to (1) effectively manage large observations through code and \oursrepl spawning, and (2) mitigate the effects of LLMs hallucinating using code.

elements.sent-2411-098: We tested \ours's ability to run under imperfect conditions in the page $k=3$ WebShop environment, using the Top-3 strategy.
elements.sent-2411-099: In order to test the error-correcting abilities of \ours, we injected minor coding mistakes into the demonstrations and tested the performance.
elements.sent-2411-100: Specifically, we injected two errors: (1) we saved variable descriptions using quotes: description = "...".
elements.sent-2411-101: This would cause errors when there are quotes in the desired description contained quotes: e.g.\ " 12" ...", and (2) we removed key variable definitions that are used later in the demonstration.
elements.sent-2411-102: We found that the LLM is relatively robust to these errors, only failing one more task on GPT-4o-mini than without any errors.
elements.sent-2411-103: We show a small sample of these corrections in \Cref{sec:bug_correct .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Next, we tested how the top-down decomposition of tasks affects the performance of \oursrepl.
elements.sent-2411-105: We created a ``No-Subtask-REPL'' version of REPL-Plan, the agent aims to solve the task without spawning any child \oursrepls.
elements.sent-2411-106: We find this strategy is conceptually similar to a code-augmented version of prior approaches, such as ReACT~\citep{yao2022react .}
elements.sent-2411-107: However, given that this approach cannot de-compose tasks, the REPL code/observation history becomes large and untenable for the agent to solve WebShop tasks.
elements.sent-2411-108: The performance decreases by around half: $52\% \to 24\%$ and $44\% \to 20\%$ respectively for GPT-3.5-instr.\ and GPT-4o-mini.
elements.sent-2411-109: As $k$-shot demonstrations are given as a pool of \oursrepls which are used in \ours, we tested the performance under the condition that one of those \oursrepls was removed from this pool, and the LLM must zero-shot infer both the task description and code writing for this \oursrepl.
elements.sent-2411-110: To do this, we removed the get\_requirements \oursrepl, which is designed to examine the item on the current page, and return an integer count of the number of matching attributes.

elements.sent-2411-116: With the increasing capabilities of LLMs, several works have proposed robust agents that interact with text-based environments (LLM-agents).

elements.sent-2411-119: However, as the LLMs tend to hallucinate incorrect actions, and incorrectly plan ahead, several works, including \ours, have been proposed that incorporate code-augmentation to LLM-agents, and recursively dividing tasks for an LLM-agent.
elements.sent-2411-120: Current LLMs (without guidance) suffer from long context understanding~\citep{li2024longcontextllmsstrugglelong and logical and arithmetic mistakes~\citep{wei2023chainofthoughtpromptingelicitsreasoning .}}

elements.sent-2411-122: For example, Program-Aided Language Models (PAL)~\citep{gao2023palprogramaidedlanguagemodels generate programs and run a Python interpreter to get solutions for mathematical, symbolic, and algorithmic reasoning problems.}
elements.sent-2411-123: LLMs and code have also been used in robotics to generate robust policies~\citep{liang2023codepolicieslanguagemodel .}
elements.sent-2411-124: Compared to these works, our work \ours focuses on the interactive decision-making setting, and focuses on how we can fix the problems of running code in these settings by creating an approach that is fully interactive (the LLM interacts with a \oursrepl to code) and top-down recursive (with spawning child \oursrepls).
elements.sent-2411-125: CodeAct and Mint~\citep{wang2024executablecodeactionselicit,wang2023mint are works that proposes an interactive LLM-code generating paradigm where the agent interacts with a human user to solve a task using code.}
elements.sent-2411-126: However, compared to \ours, these works are not designed for the LLM-agent space, and also do not split interactive code in a recursive manner.
elements.sent-2411-127: AdaPlanner~\citep{sun2023adaplanneradaptiveplanningfeedback also focuses on the planning setting by writing and refining code, but is not interactive, as a code plan must be written entirely in one pass.}
elements.sent-2411-128: In the few-shot in-context learning setting, LLMs can struggle with increased task complexity.
elements.sent-2411-129: To deal with this, ~\citet{khot2023decomposedpromptingmodularapproach have proposed to decompose challenging tasks to simpler sub-tasks and solve these individually.}
elements.sent-2411-130: ADaPT~\citep{prasad2024adaptasneededdecompositionplanning recursively decomposes complex sub-tasks as-needed to adapt to LLMs' capability and task complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-131: Prior works ~\citep{wang2023voyageropenendedembodiedagent,liu2024skillact have decomposed tasks into re-usable code blocks, or prompts, which can be re-used in new tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-132: THREAD~\citep{schroeder2024threadthinkingdeeperrecursive is a work that recursively divides LLM context into thread, which each individually solve some sub-task of the whole.}
elements.sent-2411-133: Thread also contains partial Python code executability, by saving variables and computing one-line Python statements.

elements.sent-2411-135: These two differences allow \ours to efficiently handle program contexts in many planning situations.
elements.sent-2411-136: In this work, we proposed REPL-Plan, a code-augmented planning approach for Large Language Models (LLMs) that is both dynamic and code-expressive, enabling effective top-down planning.
elements.sent-2411-137: By extending the Read-Eval-Print Loop (REPL) paradigm, we create LLM-REPLs, which allow LLMs to interactively generate code, recursively spawn child LLM-REPLs, and dynamically adapt to complex tasks.
elements.sent-2411-138: Through evaluations on diverse planning benchmarks, REPL-Plan demonstrates superior performance compared to baseline methods, showcasing its ability to handle complex, long-horizon tasks with flexibility and robustness.

elements.sent-2411-140: As we showed in the ablation experiments in \Cref{tab:ablations , LLMs have some capabilities of generalizing beyond the $k$-demonstrations (when the $k$-demonstrations contain partially buggy code, zero-shot subtask-REPL inference).}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{LLM-REPL Pseudocode} \label{alg:llmrepl} \Class{LLMREPLFunction}{ \Function{\_\_init\_\_($\var{task\_description}$)}{ $\var{task} \gets \var{task\_description}$\; $\var{history} \gets []$ \Comment{REPL code/output history} $\var{locals} \gets \{\var{get\_args, answer, act, get\_obs}\}$ \Comment{Insert REPL primitives to local state} $\var{pool} \gets$ \Pseudo{global REPL pool}\; } \Function{\_\_call\_\_($\var{*args}$)}{ \While {True} { \If {\Pseudo{need new REPL block}}{ $\var{code} \gets$ \FuncCall{QueryLLM}{$\var{task, history}$}\; } \Try{}{ \FuncCall{exec}{$\var{code}, \var{locals}$} \Comment{Return if answer is called} $\var{history.append}$(\Pseudo{format code, stdout, stderr}) \Comment{only reached if code finishes without interrupt} } \Except{$\var{NameError}$}{ \Comment{When LLM calls an unseen function} $\var{fname} \gets$ \Pseudo{get undefined variable name}\; \Pseudo{If REPL not in global REPL pool}\; $\var{subtask} \gets$ \FuncCall{QueryLLM}{$\var{fname, history}$}\; $\var{locals[fname]} \gets$ \FuncCall{LLMREPLFunction}{$\var{subtask}$}\; } \Pseudo{Return to code state after last interrupt} } } }
elements.sent-2411-147: Implementing an LLM-REPL, as described in~\Cref{alg:llmrepl , can be accomplished in various approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-148: The primary implementation challenge is how to handle child LLM-REPL spawning --- whenever there is an undefined function call, an LLM-REPL should be created and run for that undefined function.
elements.sent-2411-149: Given the undecidable nature of determining whether a function or variable is undefined~\cite{sipser1996introduction , the most flexible approach is to handle any undefined variable/function errors, NameErrors in Python, during runtime.}
elements.sent-2411-150: One approach is to modify a code interpreter (e.g.\ the Python interpreter) to automatically intercept these errors and continue execution.
elements.sent-2411-151: Continuing execution between parent and child LLM-REPLs can be synced by running different threads or co-routines with various concurrency synchronization primitives.
elements.sent-2411-152: We implemented LLM-REPL without modifying the interpreter or running any concurrency routines by saving execution state for each REPL loop iteration and re-running code when LLM-REPLs are spawned.
elements.sent-2411-153: When a NameError is encountered, we create an LLM-REPL and add it as a variable to the last saved execution state.
elements.sent-2411-154: Finally, we re-run the code from this last execution state.
elements.sent-2411-155: However, an issue with this approach is multiple context sensitive functions (other LLM-REPLs, act) can be called again after an interrupt.
elements.sent-2411-156: For example, consider the execution trace from \Cref{tab:cxt-pass .}
elements.sent-2411-157: After the NameError(count\_even), act(i*2+1) will be called again after re-running the code.
elements.sent-2411-158: Avoiding incorrect and duplicate computation can avoided by keeping a function call counter for each LLM-REPL, then when a context-sensitive function called more than once, the return value is returned using a cache.
elements.sent-2411-159: These call counters must be updated and synced between parent-child LLM-REPLs after each context-passing function.
elements.sent-2411-160: Another implementation detail is how to differentiate between a code statement that intends to spawn a new child LLM-REPL, and a code statement that makes a mistake by forgetting to define a variable.
elements.sent-2411-161: For example, consider the following lines of code:.
elements.sent-2411-162: In the case that max\_price and get\_max\_price are undefined, both lines would raise a NameError.
elements.sent-2411-163: In both cases, in our framework, an LLM-REPL will be created and named max\_price and get\_max\_price respectively.
elements.sent-2411-164: However, in the implementation of an LLM-REPL, we override any non-function method (such as comparators and string representations) to raise a custom error: REPLNameError.
elements.sent-2411-165: Because of this, the first line of code 1.\ will result in a REPLNameError raised where the LLM will be given feedback that it forgot to define the max\_price variable.
elements.sent-2411-166: The second line of code 2.\ will jump to the get\_max\_price() LLM-REPL execution.
elements.sent-2411-167: We have attached our implementation for LLM-REPLs with example test cases in the supplementary material.

elements.sent-2411-168: We found that running THREAD~\cite{schroeder2024threadthinkingdeeperrecursive on the author's codebase with newer OpenAI models such as GPT4o and GPT4o-mini tended to always cause infinite loops --- after seeing an observation, the LLMs would always generate \#START\# tokens, which indicate the start of a prompt, causing the model to re-generate the actions and text before seeing the observation, and repeat this \#START\# generation.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Under normal generation, the LLM should never generate these tokens.
elements.sent-2411-170: We believe that the GPT4o series may not listen to literal commands to follow demonstrations exactly when viewing web demonstrations, compared to GPT3.5-instruct, which was used in the original paper for web evironments such as WebShop.
elements.sent-2411-171: To correct this, we modified the algorithm $THREAD$ as follows: for each LLM generation, $G(c + Y)$, if \#START\# is a substring, we prompt the LLM to re-generate the text.
elements.sent-2411-172: If this happens, still, we then prompt using the following ``don't loop'' prompt $L = $ 'Do NOT output \#START\#.
elements.sent-2411-173: Look at the examples for what to do next.
elements.sent-2411-174: If your sub-task is finished or failed, report the status of your task with: print("<status here>") \#END\# Continue output:' We re-query the LLM with $G(c + Y + L)$, then proceed using THREAD.
elements.sent-2411-175: We find this change is able to improve THREAD from always looping on WebShop with GPT4o-mini, to a 21\% success rate.

elements.sent-2411-179: We constructed the Real-World Web Task environment by interacting with live websites on the browser.
elements.sent-2411-180: Observations are taken from the accessibility tree of the website.
elements.sent-2411-181: Each website HTML markup is represented in a browser as a tree of elements (e.g.\ links, images, buttons, etc.) called a DOM tree.
elements.sent-2411-182: The DOM tree is then converted to an accessibility tree by the browser, by adding text information to DOM nodes that may be useful for users of assistive technologies, such as screen readers.
elements.sent-2411-183: This also makes the accessibility tree readable for text-only observations given to LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-184: Our environment specifically takes the accessibility tree from the browser, then strips unnecessary nodes from the tree, and adds unique element IDs to each interactive element.
